Patch Set 4:

1. I have found some new way to handle the linkEA overflow: the timestamp will be set as the MDT's local time on which the object resides, even if it is set from remote MDT. Then it needs NOT to care about the clock drift.

2. The linkEA shrink will be handled by set_xattr sponsor.

3. More adjustment cleanup as suggested.

4. Split the patch into two parts as suggested.

Patch Set 6:

(5 comments)

wouldn't it be better to disallow new hard links?

Patch Set 6:

LinkEA's correctness is important for migration, this is why I think it's easier for us to limit the number of hardlinks.

Patch Set 6:

let's consider the following use case: there is a file with many hardlnks and the full LinkEA doesn't fit, so it's short, skipping some links. now someone wants to migrate a whole MDT to replace MDS. how this will be possible? in theory we have to scan a significan part of the whole filesystem to find links missed in LinkEA, but 1) we don't have such a functionality now 2) this can be a very lengthy process loading MDT(s) with lots of random I/O

Patch Set 6:

(2 comments)

Restrict the hard links because of linkEA size limitation may cause some POSIX tests failure. LinkEA is Lustre special, but 'nlink' attribute is more POSIX. We do not know whether there will be some application to create a lot of hard links on the same file. If it can do that on the local backend, such as EXT4, but fail to do that on Lustre, then it will affect the application to be applied on Lustre.

Currently, we will NOT allow the file with linkEA overflowed to be migrated. But since linkEA overflow is very rare case, I do NOT think it will affect the system usability too much if only very limited files cannot be migrated.

Patch Set 6:

(1 comment)

It is true that POSIX does not define the hardlinks value, it depends on the detailed filesystem implementation. But for Lustre, if we restrict that based on the EA size, then such limitation will be variable depending on the hard link names length. It is not easy to be described as Lustre spec, and not convenient to be used by the applications.

As for migration, if we want to make some MDS to retire, then device level data migration (or say backup) will be more efficient than the file-level migration, then no hardlinks trouble.

Patch Set 7:

Alex, I think the current limitation exists for migrating files today if there are too many links on a file because of the transaction size limit.  At least in this case, with the limited linkEA size then the inode itself can be migrated off the MDT and some amount of the links.

If there are other links not in linkEA on the decommissioned MDT then they should be moved as part of the directory traversal on that MDT and links on other MDTs don't really matter.

Patch Set 7:

well,  if we want to migrate MDT and some object on that MDT has short LinkEA, then we get stale direntries on other MDTs as migration changes FID, no?

Patch Set 7:

For the object with overflowed linkea, we can NOT migrate it, otherwise, it will cause dangling name entry. But consider upgrading case, the object with overflowed linkEA may exists already. We have to face and handle it. Only forbid more link() on the object (with linkEA full) is not enough.

Patch Set 7:

I think it's two different use cases. if we already have such an object with incomplete LinkEA, then it probably makes sense to run LFSCK, print a warning, something else. but allowing regular users to create hardlinks disabling migration/decomission is something different. so I asked do we consider the 2nd is OK?

Patch Set 7:

I understand your concern. But from the LFSCK view, the 1st case and 2nd case are the same. If LFSCK found overflowed linkEA, in spite of it is new formatted system or upgrading from old device, what the LFSCK can do is that adding some flag (overflow timestamp) into the linkEA header to prevent the object to be migrated, right?

Patch Set 7:

Actually, I thought that the migrate code kept a "forwarding" inode on the old MDT to handle such a case?  I'm not sure how long those entries last, at one time we discussed this, but it may be that they only last until the MDT is restarted? 

Di, do you recall the details?

If there was a need to empty an MDT completely, then it would also be possible for LFSCK to find those entries that are referencing the old MDT FID and update them to the new FID?

Patch Set 7:

> If there was a need to empty an MDT completely, then it would also be possible for LFSCK to find those entries that are referencing the old MDT FID and update them to the new FID?

If we make LFSCK to do that, then we need to record the old FID at some place. It can NOT be the old object since we want to empty the MDT totally.

Patch Set 8:

”Actually, I thought that the migrate code kept a "forwarding" inode on the old MDT to handle such a case?  I'm not sure how long those entries last, at one time we discussed this, but it may be that they only last until the MDT is restarted? 
Di, do you recall the details?“

Yes，right now we kept the “forward" inode for those un-notified client until next recovery.  see mdt_reint_open(), and check lmv_ea(LMV_HASH_FLAG_MIGRATION)
for those migrated inodes.
Patch Set 8: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 8:

One option to handle the dangling references for migrated FIDs is to keep the "FID forwarding" table longer than just until the next recovery, but until the next LFSCK run.  That would only be needed for files with hard links that are not in the linkEA list.  For MDS removal this table would need to be stored on some other MDS than the one being removed, or alternately we would need to require a full LFSCK run when removing an MDS.  That would be possible when removing an MDS cleanly, but not when the MDS dies unexpectedly.

Another option that I previously discussed with Di in LU-7607 it would also be possible to keep the original FID in the LMA after migration so that LFSCK could repair the links by matching the dirent FIDs to the original FID in the LMA.  Once LFSCK completes a run and no links are found to FIDs in the table, and the entries are old enough the entry could be removed.

If the latter proposal seems useful, it would make more sense to change the implementation here to store the overflow timestamp in a "remap index" (ZAP or IAM) that stores the target FID and/or the timestamp in a small struct, so that we can know when it can be expired from the table.  This has the advantage of consolidating the existing remapping table and moving the overflow timestamp out of the linkEA, so that it doesn't consume space in every single xattr.  That table would need to be used by LFSCK or if OI lookup returns ENOENT.  It _might_ be possible to store the remap table in the OI directly so that there is old FID->new FID mapping, but I don't know how easily that would be implemented.

Patch Set 8:

From the implementation view, if the MDT-object has overflowed linkea, and if we want to migrate such MDT-object, then we can keep the MDT-object there after the migration and record the FID of the new MDT-object in the old MDT-object's LMA EA. After the migration, run namespace LFSCK, when the namespace LFSCK finds the name entry that references the old MDT-object but the name is not in the linkEA, then the namespace LFSCK will update the name entry to reference the new MDT-object. After all these 'unknown' name entries have been handled, then the old MDT-object can be removed. If someone accesses the old MDT-object during the namespace LFSCK, then we need to re-direct the accessing to the new MDT-object, that involves some implementation detail, need to be considered well. Anyway, if it is the acceptable solution, I can make another patch based on the current patches.

Patch Set 8:

It makes sense to integrate the LFSCK handling with the redirection inodes that are already kept for DNE migration today, with the main change being that they would not be removed at the end of recovery, but rather when LFSCK has been run.

Lai, Alex, or Di, would you mind reviewing this?

commit.subject: LU-8569 linkea: linkEA size limitation
commit.message:
LU-8569 linkea: linkEA size limitation

Under DNE mode, if we do not restrict the linkEA size, and if there
are too many cross-MDTs hard links to the same object, then it will
casue the llog overflow. On the other hand, too many linkEA entries
in the linkEA will serious affect the linkEA performance because we
only support to locate linkEA entry consecutively.

So we need to restrict the linkEA size. Currently, it is 4096 bytes,
that is independent from the backend. If too many hard links caused
the linkEA overflowed, we will add overflow timestamp in the linkEA
header. Such overflow timestamp has some functionalities:

1. It will prevent the object being migrated to other MDT, because
   some name entries may be not in the linkEA, so we cannot update
   these name entries for the migration.

2. It will tell the namespace LFSCK that the 'nlink' attribute may
   be more trustable than the linkEA, then avoid misguiding the
   namespace LFSCK to repair 'nlink' attribute based on linkEA.

There will be subsequent patch(es) for namespace LFSCK to handle the
linkEA size limitation and overflow cases.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: I2d6c2be04305c1d7e3af160d8b80e73b66a36483
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/23500
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: wangdi <di.wang@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Lai Siyao <lai.siyao@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
